raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, identified as the presenter, standing at a podium on a grand, modern stage. His clear, professional voice begins by describing how films take us out of our everyday lives and allow us to walk in someone else's shoes, acknowledging that it's not always comfortable but a journey we wouldn't otherwise take. As the camera pulls back to reveal the full stage with its sleek, curved metallic silver and white patterns, he continues, noting that four of these films are directed or co-directed by women. The audience, dressed in formal attire, responds with warm, sustained applause. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage, revealing a large, circular, illuminated backdrop with a starry, cosmic design. The presenter's voice returns, emphasizing that these films will have an impact not just for today but will resonate for years to come, before announcing, "Here are the nominees for Best Documentary Feature." A cinematic montage then begins, showcasing clips from the nominated films on a curved screen. The first clip is from 'American Factory,' showing workers in a factory setting, with on-screen text listing the film's title and creators: Steven Bognar, Julia Reichert, and Jeff Reichert. A male voice from the film is heard stating, "What we're doing is we're welding two cultures together, the Chinese culture and the US culture." The next clip is from 'The Cave,' depicting a war-torn environment with rubble and fire, followed by a close-up of a hand writing a note with the phrase 'We will never give up, whatever they do,' with the film's title and creators, Feras Fayyad, Kirstine Barfod, and Sigrid Dyekjær, displayed below. A female voice from 'The Cave' clip is heard speaking in a foreign language. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout, reinforcing the celebratory and formal atmosphere of the awards ceremony. A brief male voice from another film is heard saying "The edge of democracy" at the very end, though its visual clip is not explicitly shown in this segment.

The segment opens with a cinematic montage of documentary film clips displayed on curved, glowing screens against a dark, starry backdrop with golden light streaks. A female voice, speaking in what sounds like Portuguese, introduces 'The Edge of Democracy' by Petra Costa, Joanna Natasegara, Shane Boris, and Tiago Pavan, stating, 'And 'The Edge of Democracy' was nothing but a short-lived dream,' as a protest scene with a Brazilian flag is shown. This transitions to 'For Sama' by Waad al-Kateab and Edward Watts, with a female voice speaking in what sounds like Arabic, as a cityscape with a tower and a child in a red hood appears. Next, 'Honeyland' by Ljubo Stefanov, Tamara Kotevska, and Atanas Georgiev is introduced by a female voice speaking in what sounds like Macedonian, showing a woman tending beehives with the subtitle 'They've killed all my bees. I have no bees.' The montage concludes with 'American Factory' by Steven Bognar, Julia Reichert, and Jeff Reichert, showing workers in high-visibility vests operating machinery, accompanied by a male voice speaking in what sounds like Chinese. As the music swells and applause begins, the scene cuts to the Oscars stage. A Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie stands at a podium, announcing, 'And the Oscar goes to... American Factory!' to a burst of loud cheers and applause from the formally dressed audience. A bald woman in a white shirt and glasses (Julia Reichert) and a bald man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie (Steven Bognar) are seen clapping and smiling as they walk toward the stage, which is illuminated with golden, curved architectural elements and sparkling lights. An announcer's voice identifies them, stating, 'This is the first Oscar and second nomination for Steven Bognar. The first Oscar and fourth nomination for Julia Reichert. The first win and nomination for Jeff Reichert.' The winners receive their Oscar statuettes on stage amidst other formally dressed attendees, including a woman in a red gown and a man in a black tuxedo, as the celebratory music and applause continue.

The segment opens with a bald woman, identified by on-screen text as Julia Reichert, wearing glasses and a white button-up shirt with black cuffs, smiling warmly as she walks toward a grand stage, holding a golden Oscar statuette. She is accompanied by a bald man, identified as Steven Bognar, in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, also holding an Oscar, walking beside her with a composed expression. As they approach the podium on the golden, circular architectural stage, illuminated by sparkling lights, the sounds of celebratory orchestral music and enthusiastic applause fill the air. Julia exclaims "Wow!" and then "Thank you," acknowledging the cheering audience. She begins her acceptance speech, gesturing expressively with her free hand while holding the statuette, speaking passionately about being in the presence of "our sister and brother documentarians" who "risk their lives" making stories. The on-screen text confirms the award is 'DOCUMENTARY FEATURE' for 'AMERICAN FACTORY,' listing Steven Bognar, Julia Reichert, and Jeff Reichert as winners. The camera cuts to the diverse, formally dressed audience, who continue to clap enthusiastically, including a man in a black tuxedo and a woman in a black sequined gown, smiling and applauding. Returning to the stage, Julia continues, detailing how these stories are brought to us "about hospitals being bombed in Syria, about Brazil, about Macedonia." She expresses, "We were so proud. We are inspired by you guys," before revealing that "Our film is from Ohio and China." She then playfully shouts, "Go Bucks, guys!" eliciting a slight cheer from the audience, and apologizes before concluding that the film "really could be from anywhere that people put on a uniform, a punch clock, trying to make their families have a better life," as her co-winner listens attentively. The atmosphere remains celebratory and emotional, enhanced by the golden stage lighting, with the ABC logo visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens with a woman in a white shirt and a man in a tuxedo, both holding Oscar statuettes, concluding their acceptance speech with a call for global worker unity and thanks to various individuals and organizations, including Netflix and Participant Media, as Tom Hanks and Al Pacino applaud in the audience. The stage, featuring a circular design with swirling patterns, then hosts an announcer who introduces the nominees for Best Documentary, Short Subject, accompanied by a cinematic montage of film clips. Clips from 'The Cave,' 'Honeyland,' featuring a woman lamenting the loss of her bees, and 'For Sama' are shown. The narrative shifts to the announcement of the winner for Best Documentary, Short Subject: 'Learning to Skateboard in a Warzone (If You're a Girl),' with Carol Dysinger and Elena Andreicheva accepting the award. Dysinger, in a brown leather jacket, and Andreicheva, in a silver gown, express their gratitude, with Dysinger reflecting on past encouragement and dedicating the film as a love letter to the brave girls of Afghanistan, highlighting the perseverance required and the role of teachers at NYU Film School and Skateistan in fostering courage. The ABC logo is visible throughout the celebratory awards ceremony.

Her voice is heard delivering a heartfelt speech, reflecting on her four-decade career, thanking her team including Annie Films, Molly Thompson, Adam Michelin, Lisa Renzler, Mary Manhart, Zama, and Tam, and dedicating her film to the brave girls of Afghanistan and the teachers at Skateistan who teach courage.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a bald woman with glasses, wearing a white button-up shirt with black cuffs, holding an Oscar statuette and speaking passionately into a microphone on a grand stage. Her voice is heard stating, "...harder and harder these days. And we believe that things will get better when workers of the world unite." She is then joined by a bald man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, also holding an Oscar, who takes the microphone to speak, gesturing expressively. He thanks the Academy and "everyone who trusted us to tell your story," specifically naming "Shashe Chowang," "Echen Jong, Meja Lee," "Lindsey Huts," and organizations like Netflix, Participant Media, Higher Ground Productions, and "the tough, inventive, great people of Dayton, Ohio." A woman in a red one-shoulder gown stands beside them, smiling warmly. The camera cuts to the audience, showing Tom Hanks and Al Pacino clapping and smiling, before returning to the stage where the man concludes his speech amidst building applause and cheering. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage, revealing a modern, circular design with swirling black and white patterns and illuminated rings, as the applause continues. The camera then focuses on a man in a dark suit with a bow tie, an announcer, standing at a podium, whose clear voice states, "And now, here are the nominees for Best Documentary, Short Subject." The video shifts to a cinematic montage displayed on a large screen, showcasing clips from various documentary films with background music. The announcer's voice lists nominees, including "In the Absence of Some, June, Gary the Young Star Club" and "Learning to Skateboard in a War Zone, Girl, Girl Dyson, and her..." The first clip shows a war-torn landscape with the text 'THE CAVE' and credits. The next clip features a woman in a yellow top tending to beehives, with the text 'Honeyland' and credits, where a woman's voice is heard saying, "They've killed all my bees. I have no bees," matching the on-screen subtitle. The final clip shows a child in a red hooded garment in a cityscape, with the text 'For Sama' and credits. The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

The video opens with a dynamic montage of short film clips displayed on curved, glowing screens against a dark, starry backdrop with golden light streaks. An announcer's voice introduces the nominees, stating "Life Overtakes Me, John Haptas and Christine Jamison" as a clip of a sleeping boy is shown, followed by "Walk Run Cha-Cha, Laura Nix and Colette Sanson" over a scene of a man dancing with women. The scene then transitions to a grand awards ceremony stage where a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie stands at a podium, announcing "And the winner is... Learning to Skateboard in a Warzone, if you're a girl!" The audience, dressed in formal attire, erupts in enthusiastic applause and cheers, with a woman in a brown coat and another in a silver gown seen clapping warmly. On-screen text overlays confirm this is the first Oscar nomination for Carol Dysinger and Elena Andreicheva. Carol Dysinger, wearing a brown leather jacket, and Elena Andreicheva, in a strapless silver gown and holding the Oscar statuette, walk to the stage. Dysinger speaks emotionally into the microphone, exclaiming "Oh my god... I know... I am," and recalling, "Frank Capra handed me a student Academy Award in 1977. And I thought I'd skip the hard part. I thought I would then go on to make..." while Andreicheva listens, smiling. The stage features a sleek, modern design with diagonal light patterns, and the ABC logo is visible. Promotional on-screen text like 'Buy code' with a QR code and 'Please Like & Share This Video' also appears. The atmosphere is celebratory and formal, filled with the audience's energetic reactions.

The video opens with a close-up of Carol Dysinger, a woman with gray, wavy hair and glasses, wearing a brown leather jacket over a black top, speaking expressively into a microphone on a grand stage with a dynamic, striped light backdrop. She holds a piece of paper, delivering an emotional acceptance speech, stating, "If I hadn't had that encouragement at my back back then, I wouldn't have been able to withstand the last four decades in this business," which is met with enthusiastic applause and cheering from the formally dressed audience visible in tiered rows behind her. Beside her, Elena Andreicheva, a younger woman with dark hair pulled back, in a strapless, light-colored gown with a sparkling embellished bodice, smiles warmly, occasionally adjusting her hair or looking at Carol while holding an Oscar statuette. Carol continues, thanking "Annie, Annie Films and Molly Thompson, Green Media," and "Adam Michelin, the man behind the women," along with "Lisa Renzler, cinematographer, and Mary Manhart, editor extraordinaire." She also acknowledges "Zama and Tam, our number one girl crew." The camera alternates between close-ups of Carol's face and wider shots showing both women. Carol then dedicates the film, explaining, "I've been working in Afghanistan since 2005 and this movie is my love letter to the brave girls of that country." She emphasizes the perseverance required, stating, "To get to Kabul from here took a whole lot of not quitting and teachers," specifically mentioning the kind of teachers she strives to be at NYU Film School and those at Skateistan, "the school at the heart of the film," which "teach girls courage to raise your hand." The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout the celebratory and formal awards ceremony atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: A woman with blonde hair and glasses, wearing a brown velvet jacket, confidently declared her intention to take a ramp on stage, receiving applause as a woman in a light-pink gown holding an Oscar statuette listened; a man in a black suit with a silver chain then discussed actors building a lasting body of work and nominated supporting actresses, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music. The scene transitioned into a cinematic montage featuring the ABC logo and promotional text, opening with a woman in a black sleeveless top in an office discussing the recent invention of the 'good father' concept and societal expectations for mothers, stating, "The idea of a good father was only invented like 30 years ago. We love them for their fallibility, but people absolutely don't accept those same failings in mothers." A man with a beard in a yellow patterned shirt was shown in a dimly lit room, with a voiceover defending "Richard" as "not a murderer" who "saved people's lives," followed by exclamations of "Mr. Preston!" and "Please clear the room!" The montage continued with a young boy in a brown jacket at a dinner table, surprised, responding "Yeah, I do" when asked if he wanted to tell a name, and a woman in a green top speaking intensely to him, affirming "Yeah? Yeah, yeah." In a kitchen, a woman in a blue patterned top looked down as a voice said, "Yeah," followed by a male voice stating, "Don't talk to your mother like that." The montage then rapidly shifted, showing a young woman in period attire, followed by a woman in a white blouse with a ruffled collar stating, "living or to support my family. And if we had children, they would be his, not mine. You said don't sit there and tell me that marriage is an economic proposition because it is." A modern office scene featured a blonde woman in a striped top, then a distressed woman with short blonde hair in a white t-shirt pleading, "I can't call anyone else and I just I need someone right now to tell me that that's what I should do." A woman in a black dress at a formal event questioned, "How do you keep defending these people?" to which a calm male voice responded, "I'm not defending, I'm just explaining," prompting the retort, "Well, stop it!" The montage included a Nazi-era town square with a young boy in a Hitler Youth uniform and a woman in a patterned jacket, where a sympathetic female voice stated, "I feel sorry for you, I really do," met with a defensive male reply, "You don't have to feel sorry for me. You're being treated the same way I am treated," and a sad female lament, "No, I'd be respected if I could be loved." Returning to modern settings, a woman with blonde hair in a white top in a bedroom declared, "They don't know anything about being a woman. You look a tiger in the eye and trust without fear. That's what it is to be a woman," adding sarcastically, "I get it from my ex, some narcissistic artist. And verbally abusive." Another woman, with short blonde hair in a black top in an office, reflected, "It would have been nice if somebody told us that he left her more than legs," before telling Kayla, "It's nobody's job to protect you, Kayla," trailing off with "That's all of our." The segment concluded with a man in a dark suit on a stage before a large, curved screen displaying black-and-white images of women, as a whooshing sound effect preceded a dramatic orchestral crescendo, with the ABC logo and promotional text consistently visible.

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens with a large, curved screen displaying black-and-white images of women as a man in a dark suit on stage announces the nominees for performance by an actress in a supporting role, accompanied by background music and building applause. The camera cuts to the audience, showing Kathy Bates smiling and clapping, Laura Dern in a black strapless gown clapping warmly, Scarlett Johansson in a shimmering silver gown reacting joyfully, Florence Pugh in a green dress beaming, and Margot Robbie in a strapless black gown clapping with an engaged look, as each is announced for their respective films. A graphic overlay lists the nominees and their film titles. The man on stage builds suspense before declaring Laura Dern the winner for "Marriage Story," prompting loud applause and cheering as she stands, smiles, waves, and walks toward the stage. A female announcer notes this is Laura Dern's first Oscar and third nomination, while Scarlett Johansson also rises with a radiant expression. The video then transitions to a well-lit awards ceremony where Laura Dern, in a black sequined gown, walks onto the stage with a man in a black tuxedo and receives the Oscar statuette. She steps to the microphone, holding the golden statuette, and begins an emotional acceptance speech, thanking "the Academy for this honor" and expressing gratitude "to be in this room with such remarkable artists, including my fellow nominees, my sisters and colleagues." On-screen text confirms the award for 'ACTRESS IN A SUPPORTING ROLE LAURA DERN "MARRIAGE STORY"'. Laura Dern continues her speech, thanking "Netflix, my friend Ted, Scott, everyone for their support and love," and specifically "Noah" (Baumbach) for "his vision, his magic," and "his words and for your friendship and art and life with you and Greta." She also thanks "this cast, the majestic Adam, Scarlett, Alan, Ray, our amazing crew," noting that "Noah wrote a movie about love and about breaching divisions in the name and in the honor of family and home." The stage features a sleek, modern design with diagonal silver lighting patterns, and the ABC logo is visible throughout. She concludes by mentioning "my stepchildren CJ and Harris, my heart and inspiration." The atmosphere is celebratory and formal, with sustained, enthusiastic applause, the audience's reactions including Scarlett Johansson, Margot Robbie, and Florence Pugh clapping and smiling, and the golden glow of the Oscar statuette emphasizing the significance of the moment.

The atmosphere is celebratory and formal, with the audience’s applause and the golden glow of the Oscar statuette emphasizing the significance of the moment.

event_blocks.summary: Laura Dern, adorned in a black sequined gown and diamond earrings, emotionally accepted her Oscar on a grand stage with a silver backdrop, dedicating the award to her parents, Diane Ladd and Bruce Dern, and calling it the 'best birthday present ever' as orchestral music swelled and the audience, including Tom Hanks and Al Pacino, applauded. The scene shifted to a stage with a starry blue backdrop where a man in a tuxedo and a woman in a white gown introduced nominees for Sound Editing, highlighting their work on 'First World War trenches,' 'The Manson Ranch and the city of Gotham,' 'The Le Mans racetrack,' and 'a galaxy far, far away,' before a cinematic montage showcased clips from 'Ford v Ferrari,' 'Joker,' '1917,' and 'Once Upon a Time in Hollywood,' accompanied by their distinct soundscapes. The 'Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker' sequence with underwater sounds preceded the announcement of 'Ford versus Ferrari' as the winner for Sound Editing, with Donald Sylvester accepting the award and thanking his sound team, the picture team, mixers, and his wife of 34 years, noting it was likely the last film from 20th Century Fox. Throughout these segments, the ABC logo was visible, interspersed with promotional text overlays and QR codes.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of Laura Dern, radiant in a black sequined gown and sparkling diamond earrings, holding her Oscar statuette as she delivers an emotional acceptance speech on a grand stage with a wavy silver backdrop. Her voice, filled with gratitude, is heard saying, "some say never meet your heroes, but I say if you're really blessed, you get them as your parents." She shares the award with her "acting heroes, my legends, Diane Ladd and Bruce Dern," as warm applause and cheering erupt from the audience, which includes Tom Hanks and Al Pacino. Laura continues, expressing her love and thanks, calling the Oscar "the best birthday present ever" and affirming, "You lift me up every day." Celebratory orchestral music swells as she triumphantly declares, "I can now officially say that I've held an Oscar on this stage." The scene then transitions to a new segment where a man in a black tuxedo and a woman in a white one-shoulder gown with a jeweled headpiece stand at a microphone on a stage with a deep blue, starry backdrop. A male voice, likely the host, is heard saying "Congratulations" and then beginning to speak about "The seemingly impossible task of sound editors is to immerse the viewer in a world they..." The segment opens on a grand stage with a blue, starry backdrop, where a woman in a white one-shoulder gown with a jeweled headpiece speaks passionately into a microphone. Her articulate voice introduces the nominees, vividly describing how they've brought to life "First World War trenches." Beside her, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie listens attentively, then adds in his clear voice, "The Manson Ranch and the city of Gotham." The woman continues, mentioning "The Le Mans racetrack," and the man concludes with "and even a galaxy far, far away. And yet so close." The camera pulls back to reveal a large audience and a curved screen above them displaying 'SOUND EDITING' in illuminated letters. As celebratory music and applause swell, the man, acting as the host, announces, "Here are the nominees for achievement in Sound Editing." A cinematic montage then showcases clips with dynamic, fast-paced editing. The host's voice introduces "Ford versus Ferrari, Donald Sylvester," accompanied by intense car race sounds of revving engines and screeching tires from a high-speed chase. Next, he announces "Joker, Alan Robert Murray," as chaotic crowd noise and subway sounds underscore a man in clown makeup dancing wildly. "1917, Oliver Tarney and Rachel Tate" is presented, with the visual of a soldier running through a war-torn field enhanced by realistic war sounds, gunfire, explosions, and a biplane engine. Finally, "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, Wylie Stateman" features explosive sounds from a flamethrower igniting a massive blast. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout, and the host begins to announce "Star Wars, The Rise of..." before the segment concludes.

The segment opens with dynamic, fast-paced underwater sounds accompanying a 'Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker' sequence featuring a dark, submerged vehicle with glowing red and blue lights. The scene transitions to a formal awards ceremony stage with a deep blue, starry backdrop, where a woman in a white one-shoulder gown with a jeweled headpiece announces, "And the Oscar goes to...", followed by a man in a black tuxedo with a beard excitedly exclaiming, "Ford versus Ferrari!", as the audience erupts in cheers and applause. A clip from 'Ford v Ferrari' then shows a high-speed nighttime car race with intense engine revving and tire screeching sounds, followed by a shot of the audience clapping enthusiastically. The woman's voice then adds, "This is the first Oscar and nomination for Donald Sylvester," as celebratory music begins. Donald Sylvester, a man with gray hair in a black tuxedo and bow tie, walks to the stage, accepts the Oscar for Sound Editing, and delivers an emotional acceptance speech, stating, "Ford versus Ferrari is a marriage of sound and picture. So all I really want to thank my fantastic sound team. I also want to include the picture team and the mixers. In fact, Ford, yeah, Ford versus Ferrari is probably the last film ever made by 20th Century Fox. So, the real support comes from home, so I want to thank my wonderful wife of 34 years, who gave up her editing career for me to pursue my career." Throughout the segment, on-screen text identifies the film and winner, and promotional overlays appear, including a 'Buy code' QR code and a 'Don't forget to Facebook Chumo Dam Subscribe' message, with an 'abc' logo visible in the bottom right corner. The atmosphere transitions from cinematic intensity to celebratory formality, culminating in the winner’s heartfelt moment on stage.

event_blocks.summary: The presentation for Sound Mixing began with a humorous anecdote about cinematographers, delivered by a man in a black tuxedo and a woman in a navy blue gown, eliciting audience laughter and applause, while promotional text and QR codes intermittently appeared on screen. The man, adjusting his earpiece, expressed confusion as he was being fed incorrect information about the cinematographer's role, leading to a comedic exchange with the woman and further audience amusement. The award itself was presented to Mark Taylor and Stuart Wilson for their work on '1917', with clips from the film, 'Ad Astra', 'Ford v Ferrari', and 'Joker' shown during the nominations. Stuart Wilson, accepting the award, thanked the '1917' sound team, his family, producers, and director Sam Mendes, acknowledging it as his first Oscar and sixth nomination, while Mark Taylor thanked his team, editor Lee Smith, the composers, and Goldcrest. The ceremony, marked by enthusiastic applause and appearances by Tom Hanks and Al Pacino, featured a blue, starry stage backdrop with swirling light patterns, and the ABC logo was consistently visible in the bottom right corner.

The segment opens with a close-up of Stuart Wilson, a man in a black tuxedo, holding an Oscar statuette, before transitioning to a wide shot of him and Mark Taylor, both in tuxedos, on stage against a deep blue, starry backdrop with swirling light patterns. Stuart Wilson, wearing glasses and a bow tie, speaks into the microphone with a British accent, thanking the entire '1917' sound team for their 'outstanding work,' including 'my guys in the trenches' and 'Mark's team in the studio,' as a lower-third graphic identifies them as 'SOUND MIXING MARK TAYLOR, STUART WILSON "1917"'. He continues, expressing gratitude for 'incredible support,' mentioning families like Victoria and Ava, producers Callum, Jane, and Pippa, and finally Sam Mendes, stating, 'None of us would be here without you. Thank you.' Loud applause and cheering erupt from the audience, with the camera cutting to show Tom Hanks clapping warmly, followed by Al Pacino and others applauding. The scene returns to the stage where Mark Taylor, also in a tuxedo with glasses, takes the microphone, holding the Oscar, and speaks with a British accent, thanking 'Ollie and his great team,' Rachel, Mike Fenton, Jambo, Lee Smith (their editor), 'all the guys who gave us such a great score to work with,' Robbie and the guys at Goldcrest, and Rob Carson, as applause briefly swells again. The camera then pulls back to a wide shot of the two winners standing with a woman in a white gown, likely a presenter, before cutting to a new pair of presenters: a woman in a navy blue gown with a diamond necklace and a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, holding a card. The woman in the navy blue gown speaks with a clear, authoritative tone, mentioning 'Jodie and Louis drivers' and having 'made multiple films' with 'an enormous amount of experience on film sets,' emphasizing understanding 'the importance' and 'primacy.' Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and on-screen promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE', and a QR code with 'Buy code' appear intermittently, indicating promotional content. The minute opens with a black screen featuring a QR code labeled 'Buy code', quickly transitioning to a formal awards ceremony stage. A man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and a woman in a navy blue spaghetti-strap gown adorned with a large diamond necklace stand at a podium. The woman, speaking with a dramatic, performative tone, begins to present, humorously detailing the non-filming duties of a 'cinematographer,' such as preparing meals and knocking on trailer doors, which elicits chuckles and applause from the audience. Intermittently, on-screen promotional text like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appears, along with another QR code. The man then takes over, continuing the comedic description of the 'cinematographer' waiting in a golf cart, also drawing audience laughter and applause. The camera briefly cuts to the audience, showing a man in a tuxedo smiling and pointing towards the stage, accompanied by a woman in a sparkly gown. Returning to the presenters, the woman concludes her part, honoring these 'unsung cinematic heroes,' again met with audience appreciation. However, the man, touching his ear as if adjusting an earpiece, interjects with a confused 'Wait. What?' and states he's 'being told that...' The woman, looking at him with concern, prompts 'What?', to which he questions if 'That is not the role of the cinematographer?' The woman responds with a surprised 'Oh,' as the man adds that it 'has something to do with camera.' The woman, now clearly confused, asks 'Who who is talking to you?', and the man, looking flustered, replies, 'I I have no idea,' as the audience's laughter and applause continue, highlighting the unexpected turn in the presentation. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

event_blocks.summary: At a grand awards ceremony, a man in a tuxedo and a woman in a navy blue gown presented the award for cinematography. After a playful exchange about earpieces and formal introductions, they announced the nominees: 'The Irishman,' 'Joker,' 'The Lighthouse,' '1917,' and 'Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.' A cinematic montage of clips from these films played, showcasing diverse visual styles, with on-screen text identifying each film and cinematographer. The presenter then declared Roger Deakins the winner for '1917,' prompting enthusiastic applause from the audience, which included Tom Hanks and Al Pacino. Roger Deakins, visibly emotional, accepted the Oscar statuette from the presenter. In his acceptance speech, he thanked his fellow nominees and acknowledged the collaborative effort behind '1917,' specifically mentioning his wife, James, and his focus puller, Andy Harris, whom he humorously described as a 'pinball wizard of focus pulling.' The ABC logo was visible throughout the segment, alongside intermittent promotional text and QR codes.

Standing at the microphone, visibly emotional, he adjusts his glasses and touches his face, beginning his acceptance speech with a calm, slightly raspy voice, saying "Thank you all." He continues, thanking his "fellow nominees for their wonderfully inspiring work" and their "friendship." A humorous aside, "I'm pretty good cook actually," elicits chuckles from the audience. He dedicates the award, stating it "belongs to a lot of people that worked on this movie," specifically mentioning his wife James and his assistant and focus puller, Andy Harris, who is "somewhere out there." This mention sparks renewed applause and cheers. Deakins praises Harris as the "pinball wizard of focus pulling," adding with a touch of humor that "you'll never know what he does... you only know he makes a mistake and he never does," drawing more appreciative laughter and applause before his speech is cut off as he mentions "Peter Cavacuti." Throughout, the ABC logo is visible, and further promotional text like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear.

event_blocks.summary: At a formal awards ceremony, a presenter in a black tuxedo and bow tie, alongside a woman in a navy blue gown, humorously misinterprets the role of a cinematographer before the actual nominees for cinematography are announced. The nominees include Roger Deakins for '1917.' The award is presented to Roger Deakins, who walks to the stage amidst loud applause from the audience, which includes Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt. Deakins, visibly emotional, accepts the Oscar, thanking his fellow nominees and his wife, James, describing his focus puller, Andy Harris, as a "wizard of focus pulling" who never makes mistakes, eliciting laughter. He also thanks operators Peter Cavacuti and Charlie Rizik, his grip crew, and his gaffer and friend of 30 years, John Higgins, concluding by thanking his "wonderful team." The stage features a modern design with light streaks and a starry backdrop, with a large 'CINEMATOGRAPHY' sign above the podium and the ABC logo in the corner. Intermittent on-screen promotional text also appears throughout the segment.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dark, blurred transition, accompanied by a whimsical, magical sound effect and light applause, before revealing a formal awards ceremony stage. A man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and a woman in a navy blue spaghetti-strap gown with a large diamond necklace stand at a podium, presenting an award. The man, holding a card, speaks in a clear male voice, humorously stating, "And it is the cinematographer and only the cinematographer who is waiting patiently for you in that golf cart to travel you to set." The woman listens attentively, occasionally glancing at him, then responds in a clear, slightly higher-pitched voice, "Oh, how splendid! Then to honor these unsung cinematic heroes." The man adjusts his earpiece, saying "Wait, I'm sorry. I'm being told that..." while the woman follows suit, touching her ear and interjecting "What?" The man continues, "That is not the role of the cinematographer," prompting an "Oh" from the woman, and then adds, "And it has something to do with camera." The woman asks, "Who, who is talking to you?" to which the man replies, "I, I have no idea. They just gave me an earpiece back. Do you not have an earpiece?" The woman checks her ear, saying "Um... Hello? Hello? No, I, I don't think anything is in there," eliciting laughter from the audience. The camera cuts to the audience, showing Brad Pitt in a black velvet tuxedo and a blonde woman in a sparkly black dress, both smiling and looking up at the stage, amidst the light background music and audience reactions. The scene returns to the presenters, who continue their interaction, with the man gesturing and the woman reacting with subtle expressions, before she announces, "Here are the nominees for achievement in cinematography," with the man echoing "The nominees..." as his voice fades. The stage backdrop features a modern, curved design with horizontal light strips, and the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner. The audience is seated in tiered rows, and the overall atmosphere is formal and celebratory, underscored by the light, whimsical background music and intermittent applause.

event_blocks.summary: Roger Deakins, holding an Oscar statuette, delivered an emotional acceptance speech on a grand stage, expressing gratitude for the "most wonderful experience" he'll "never forget," met with audience applause and cheering. The scene shifted to presenters, a Woman in a Navy Blue Spaghetti-Strap Gown and a Man in a Black Tuxedo, who humorously discussed film editors' work before announcing the nominees for Film Editing. A dynamic montage displayed clips from 'Ford v Ferrari,' 'The Irishman,' 'Jojo Rabbit,' 'Joker,' and 'Parasite,' accompanied by on-screen text and audience applause. The Woman in Navy Blue Gown then announced Michael McCusker and Andrew Buckland as the winners for 'Ford v Ferrari,' noting it was their first Oscar win. McCusker, thanking the Academy, fellow nominees, his wife Maria, son Lucas, sister Claire, and parents, expressed his love for his family in Argentina. Buckland praised director James Mangold, the cast, DP Phedon Papamichael, producers, and everyone at 20th Century. The segment concluded with a clip from 'Ford v Ferrari' showing the winners beside a vintage car on a runway, with their names displayed on screen, while the ABC logo was consistently visible.

event_blocks.summary: The event segment begins with a blurred transition revealing two men in black tuxedos holding Oscar statuettes on stage, celebrating their win amidst enthusiastic applause and uplifting orchestral music. One man, with dark hair, a beard, and glasses, thanks "tireless Aaron Downey, Teca Liano, Steve Asbell, and the inimitable Emma Watts," and expresses love for his daughter "Lena" and "Bob." The camera then cuts to the audience, showing two women, one in a black dress and another in a blue off-the-shoulder gown, clapping. The scene shifts back to the stage as the winners depart, and David Rubin, identified as casting director and Academy President, ascends the stage. Rubin, wearing a black tuxedo, glasses, and bow tie, stands at a podium against a backdrop of swirling, metallic silver patterns, speaking with expressive hand gestures about the event being the Academy's highlight, a milestone, and mentioning the history of the Academy's museum founded under Douglas Fairbanks, before a "special announcement." The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner. David, in a black tuxedo with round, dark-rimmed glasses, stands on a grand, futuristic stage with illuminated white lines and a dynamic, swirling silver and blue background, as an upbeat orchestral score plays. He describes a "state-of-the-art showcase of moviemaking" and introduces "two-time Oscar winner, Tom Hanks," eliciting enthusiastic audience applause and cheers. Tom Hanks, also in a black tuxedo, walks onto the stage, shakes David’s hand, and takes the microphone. Hanks thanks David and delivers a humorous speech, punctuated by audience laughter and applause, about museums in the "City of Angels," including one for selfies, and emphasizes the significance of a new museum for motion pictures, humorously stating he was "putting up some drywall" there. Promotional text such as 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appears in the top left, while the ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner, all under bright, focused lighting.

The segment opens with a man, identified as David, wearing round, dark-rimmed glasses and a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing on a grand, futuristic stage with illuminated white lines and a dynamic, swirling silver and blue background. An upbeat, orchestral score plays as David speaks with a composed and expressive demeanor, gesturing as he describes a “state-of-the-art showcase of moviemaking.” He then introduces “two-time Oscar winner, Tom Hanks,” prompting enthusiastic audience applause and cheers. Tom Hanks, also in a black tuxedo, walks onto the stage, shakes David’s hand, and takes the microphone. He thanks David and delivers a humorous speech, punctuated by audience laughter and applause, about the diverse museums in the “City of Angels,” including one dedicated to selfies, before emphasizing the significance of a new museum for motion pictures, humorously adding that he was “putting up some drywall” there. Throughout the segment, the camera alternates between close-ups of the speakers and wider stage shots, while promotional text like 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appears in the top left, and the ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner, all under bright, focused lighting.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage with a dynamic, abstract background of swirling, metallic patterns, the Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and glasses, humorously referenced "Scarlett Johansson" and "Brad Pitt was on the roof working with his shirt off," adding, "As was Colin Jost," which prompted audience laughter and applause, followed by his statement, "And dude, that was no contest." The ABC logo was visible in the bottom right corner, and promotional text reading 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appeared in the top left. The camera shifted between close-ups of the Host and wider shots, then focused on the audience, where a woman in a red sequined gown and another in a brown sleeveless top were seen clapping, amplifying the audible applause. The Host then proudly announced, "We're all very proud of what has been accomplished so far in the landmark that is taking shape at Fairfax and Wilshire... And it is a pleasure to announce that the Academy Museum of Motion Pictures will open its doors on December 14th of 2020," receiving significant applause and the commencement of background music. The scene transitioned to a comedic stage performance featuring a Man in Black Cat Costume, wearing a top hat, and a Woman in Orange Fox Costume, both dancing and gesturing humorously in front of a microphone on a circular stage illuminated by warm, glowing lights, with the music and audience applause continuing. The Host's voice briefly interjected, mentioning "As cos members of the Motion Picture Academy," during the performance.

event_blocks.summary: The segment commenced with a female announcer's voice emphasizing the significance of visual effects, followed by audience applause and a shift to a comedic stage performance featuring a man in a black cat costume and a woman in an orange fox costume dancing under golden lights. The camera then panned across the awards stage and audience, focusing on a woman in a silver gown clapping, before the announcer introduced the nominees for achievement in visual effects. A montage showcased behind-the-scenes and cinematic clips from nominated films: 'Avengers: Endgame,' with its team Dan DeLeeuw, Russell Earl, Matt Aitken, and Dan Sudick, displayed alongside motion-capture and green-screen sequences; 'The Irishman,' featuring its team Pablo Helman, Leandro Estebecorena, Nelson Sepulveda-Fauser, and Stephane Grabli, with a split-screen clip; 'The Lion King,' listing Robert Legato, Adam Valdez, Andrew R. Jones, and Elliot Newman, accompanied by an animated scene; '1917,' naming Guillaume Rocheron, Greg Butler, and Dominic Tuohy, with a dramatic war-torn landscape; and 'Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker,' announcing Roger Guyett, Neal Scanlan, Patrick Tubach, and Dominic Tuohy, alongside a close-up of a decaying face, with the ABC logo visible throughout. Later, a whimsical score accompanied the cat and fox costumed performers, transitioning to a dramatic montage of '1917' before the female announcer declared it the winner for Visual Effects. Guillaume Rocheron, Greg Butler, and Dominic Tuohy accepted the Oscar, with on-screen text confirming their win. Rocheron, speaking at the podium, thanked the Academy, described '1917' as a 'dream project' that challenged visual effects approaches and fostered collaboration, and began thanking Sam Mendes, while promotional overlays and the ABC logo remained visible.

raw_segments: The segment opens with dynamic, blurred warm lights, transitioning to a close-up of a woman in a shimmering silver gown laughing joyfully in the audience, as a female announcer's voice declares with clear, professional diction, "Nobody more than us understands the importance of good visual effects." This is followed by a burst of applause and cheering from the audience, as the scene quickly shifts to a comedic stage performance featuring a man in a black cat costume and a woman in an orange fox costume dancing playfully with exaggerated movements against a backdrop of golden, glowing lights. The camera then cuts to a wide, sweeping aerial view of the grand, circular awards stage and the vast, seated audience, before focusing on a woman in a sparkling orange sequined gown, smiling warmly and clapping amidst the continued applause. The announcer then states, "Here are the nominees for achievement in visual effects," accompanied by more applause, as the segment transitions into a high-energy montage of behind-the-scenes and cinematic clips from nominated films. The first film, 'Avengers: Endgame,' is introduced by the announcer naming its visual effects team – "Dan DeLeeuw, Russell Earl, Matt Aitken, and Dan Sudick" – while showing a man in a motion-capture suit, another in a blue robe, and a green-screen action sequence, with the team's names also appearing on-screen. Next, 'The Irishman' is presented with its team – "Pablo Helman, Leandro Estebecorena, Nelson Sepulveda-Fauser, and Stephane Grabli" – alongside a split-screen clip of a man in a red fez, with the names displayed on-screen. 'The Lion King' follows, with the announcer listing "Robert Legato, Adam Valdez, Andrew R. Jones, and Elliot Newman" as its visual effects team, as a black-and-white animated scene featuring giraffes, a zebra, and a warthog plays, also showing the names on-screen. Then, '1917' is introduced with "Guillaume Rocheron, Greg Butler, and Dominic Tuohy" as its team, showing a dramatic, silhouetted figure in a war-torn landscape with a cross and burning buildings, with the names on-screen. The montage concludes with 'Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker,' its team – "Roger Guyett, Neal Scanlan, Patrick Tubach, and Dominic Tuohy" – announced as a close-up of a decaying, gray face with smoke and debris is shown, with the names on-screen. Throughout this celebratory sequence, which highlights the technical artistry of the nominees, the ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner, and the segment ends with continued applause.

The segment opens with a whimsical, upbeat musical score, featuring xylophone-like sounds and what resembles light applause, accompanying a comedic performance on a grand stage. A man in a black cat costume with a top hat and white face paint, and a woman in an orange-and-white fox costume with fluffy ears and tail, perform a playful, exaggerated dance under warm golden lights. The scene briefly shifts to an audience, showing a man in a black tuxedo shaking hands with another, while a woman in a teal gown smiles. This transitions into a dramatic cinematic montage from the film '1917', depicting soldiers in a war-torn battlefield under a gray sky with biplanes overhead, all while the whimsical music continues. The scene then returns to the awards stage, where a female announcer's voice declares, "And the Oscar goes to... 1917!" This is met with loud applause and cheering. The announcer continues, identifying the winners: "This is the second Oscar and second nomination for Guillaume Rocheron, and first win and second nomination for Greg Butler, and the first win and third nomination for Dominic Tuohy." Three men in black tuxedos—Guillaume Rocheron, Greg Butler, and Dominic Tuohy—walk onto the stage, holding Oscar statuettes, as the on-screen text confirms their win for 'Visual Effects' for '1917'. They stand together at the podium against a golden, circular backdrop with sparkling lights. The whimsical music continues as Guillaume Rocheron steps forward to speak, expressing gratitude: "Well, thank you for the Academy for this tremendous honor." He describes '1917' as "what you call a dream project," an "opportunity to challenge how we approach visual effects," and a "chance to collaborate with some truly fantastic people," before beginning to thank "Sam Mendes." The ABC logo and promotional overlays ('PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', 'Buy code' QR) are visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The awards ceremony commenced with a black screen displaying 'THANKS' before transitioning to a brightly lit stage where a man in a tuxedo, holding an Oscar statuette, expressed gratitude to his producers, wife Kate, and the 600 artists at MPC and the special effects team, emphasizing shared victory, followed by a brief "Thank you" from another voice. The audience, including a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown and a woman with short gray hair, watched. Charlize Theron, in a pink one-shoulder gown, joined a man in a tuxedo at the microphone to present the Makeup and Hairstyling award, humorously remarking on her age before introducing herself. A host in a tuxedo recounted his amazement at the transformative hair and makeup in 'The Irishman,' to which Theron playfully responded about the authenticity of the transformations. As orchestral music swelled, Theron announced the nominees, and a montage featured clips from 'Bombshell' (Kazu Hiro, Anne Morgan, Vivian Baker), 'Joker' (Nicki Lederman, Kay Georgiou), 'Judy' (Jeremy Woodhead), 'Maleficent: Mistress of Evil' (Paul Gooch, Arjen Tuiten, David White), and '1917' (Naomi Donne, Tristan Versluis, Rebecca Cole), with each film and team identified. The montage concluded with the nominees for '1917' displayed on a large screen, accompanied by loud audience applause, as the host prepared to announce the winner.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a black screen displaying the word 'THANKS' before transitioning to a grand awards ceremony stage, illuminated with golden lights and a sparkling circular backdrop. Three men in black tuxedos stand holding Oscar statuettes. The central figure, a man with a beard and dark hair (Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Winner)), speaks into a microphone, expressing heartfelt gratitude to their producers, his beautiful wife Kate for standing by him, and the team of 600 artists at MPC and the fantastic special effects team, emphasizing that they share this award with them. He concludes with a sincere "Thank you." Another voice briefly adds, "Thank you all, thank you so much... thank you to the team, thank you." The camera cuts to the audience, showing a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown with an updo, smiling warmly, and later, a woman with short gray hair and large hoop earrings listening intently beside a young man in a tuxedo. The scene then shifts to a different part of the stage where a woman in a shimmering light-pink one-shoulder gown (Woman in Pink Gown) and a man in a black tuxedo stand at a microphone. The woman speaks passionately, greeting the audience with "Good evening" and announcing they are there to present the award for Makeup and Hairstyling, highlighting the impossible task of overstating the role hair and makeup play in filmmaking. She then humorously adds, "Case in point? I'm 87 years old," followed by audience laughter, before introducing herself with "And I'm Charlize Theron." Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and the celebratory atmosphere is underscored by audience applause and reactions, including a woman in a black dress clapping and smiling, her blonde hair in a bun. The stage backdrop features dynamic, wavy silver patterns with warm lighting.

The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, identified as a host, speaking at a podium. In a clear, confident, and slightly humorous voice, he recounts, "Listen, all joking aside, I do want to say, I saw firsthand how talented hair and makeup people are in The Irishman. They would transform us every day. I would just sit there and be amazed." He then adds, "And then Pachi would come in and say..." before trailing off. A quick transition shows a woman in a shimmering light-pink one-shoulder gown standing beside him, also a host, listening attentively. She playfully interjects, "I, I think they're gonna believe that. Not everything is Netflix, right?" to which the man responds, "Sorry. Yeah. I got carried away." She then adds, "Everyone woke up there." As light orchestral music begins to swell, the woman formally announces, "Here are the wonderfully talented artists nominated this year for achievement in makeup and hairstyling." The camera cuts to a wide shot of the grand stage, revealing a large, curved screen above them displaying 'MAKEUP AND HAIRSTYLING' in illuminated letters, set against golden architectural elements. The scene transitions into a cinematic montage showcasing the nominees, with the woman's voice narrating each film title and its makeup and hairstyling team. Quick cuts display film clips: a woman in a black-and-white patterned top from 'Bombshell', with the woman's voice identifying "Kazu Hiro, Anne Morgan, and Vivian Baker"; a man in clown makeup from 'Joker', with the woman's voice identifying "Nicki Lederman and Kay Georgiou"; a woman in a red dress from 'Judy', with the woman's voice identifying "Jeremy Woodhead"; a woman with dark, dramatic makeup from 'Maleficent: Mistress of Evil', with the woman's voice identifying "Paul Gooch, Arjen Tuiten, and David White"; and a soldier in a muddy trench from '1917', with the woman's voice identifying "Naomi Donne, Tristan Versluis, and Rebecca Cole." Each clip is accompanied by on-screen text confirming the film title and team. The montage concludes with a final shot of the stage, where the nominees for '1917' are displayed on the screen, and the audience is visible in the background, clapping loudly as the orchestral music continues. The ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner throughout, and the man begins to speak again, saying, "And the..." as the minute ends.

event_blocks.summary: The awards ceremony stage, adorned with a swirling golden and silver backdrop and warm stage lighting, hosts the Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Host) and the Woman in Pink Gown. The host announces "Bombshell" as the winner for MAKEUP AND HAIRSTYLING, noting it as Kazu Hiro's second Oscar and fourth nomination, prompting enthusiastic applause and cheering from the audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Gown and the Woman with Short Gray Hair and Hoop Earrings. Kazu Hiro, the Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Winner), walks to the podium flanked by the Woman in Black Dress and the Woman in Blue Gown, both holding Oscar statuettes. At the podium, Kazu Hiro delivers an earnest acceptance speech, thanking the Academy members, director Jay Roach, various crew members, Fox Studio, Lionsgate, friends, family, and mentors Rick Baker and Dick Smith. He expresses "heartfelt thanks to Charisse Theron," calling her "amazing" and crediting her "bravery and passion" for the film's success and setting a new bar in the makeup industry. The Woman in Black Dress, in a black sleeveless gown with a plunging neckline and glasses, and the Woman in Blue Gown, in a strapless royal blue gown with dangling earrings, stand attentively beside him, holding their statuettes. On-screen text confirms the award category and winners, while promotional overlays and the ABC logo are visible. The celebratory atmosphere is palpable with the audience clapping and smiling.

Promotional overlays like 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'WATCH', and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appear in the top left corner, alongside the ABC logo in the bottom right, while the celebratory and formal atmosphere is enhanced by warm stage lighting.

event_blocks.summary: The segment commenced with a man in a black tuxedo emotionally accepting an award, followed by a wide shot of a grand stage where three winners, two women and one man, held Oscar statuettes amidst audience applause; a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown was seen clapping enthusiastically before the winners departed. Dramatic, upbeat music swelled as an announcer previewed performances by Penelope Cruz and Elton John, and upcoming Oscar presentations for Directing, Best Actor, Actress, and Best Picture, with the illuminated 'OSCARS' structure and 'The Oscars on ABC, brought to you by Cadillac' text displayed. A woman in a black gown with a large white bow then announced the nominees for Best International Feature Film, leading into a montage of clips from 'Corpus Christi' (Poland), 'Honeyland' (North Macedonia), and 'Les Misérables' (France), with on-screen text detailing titles, countries, and directors, all under the visible ABC logo. The narrative shifted to clips of 'Pain and Glory' and 'Parasite,' with the 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE FILM' sign glowing on stage as the Woman in Black Gown with White Bow declared, "And the Oscar goes to... Parasite!" eliciting cheers from Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt. She noted this was the first win for South Korea, after which Bong Joon Ho and Han Jin Won, co-winners, celebrated before proceeding to the stage. Bong Joon Ho, wearing a dark suit and glasses, accepted the Oscar statuette with Han Jin Won and a Woman in Black Outfit, delivering an emotional speech in English and Korean, expressing gratitude and triumph as celebratory orchestral music played. Bong Joon Ho, on stage with Han Jin Won, continued his speech, thanking actors, staff, cinematographer Hong Kyung-pyo, production designer Lee Ha-jun, editor Yang Jin-mo, and production companies Barunson, CJ, and Neon, while Han Jin Won offered support, all under the ABC logo and amidst fan-edited promotional overlays.

Her clear voice is heard announcing nominees 'Pain and Glory' and 'Parasite,' then declaring 'Parasite' as the winner, and providing context that it's the first South Korean film to be nominated and win the Oscar.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, appearing emotional as he delivers an acceptance speech, expressing gratitude and stating, "we wouldn't be getting this award without you." The scene quickly transitions to a wide shot of a grand awards stage with a circular, illuminated backdrop featuring intricate, swirling patterns, where three winners—two women and one man—stand holding Oscar statuettes amidst the enthusiastic clapping and cheering of the formally dressed audience. A close-up shows a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown smiling and clapping enthusiastically, before the winners walk off stage. Dramatic, upbeat music swells as an announcer's voice highlights upcoming performances, including Penelope Cruz and Elton John performing "Rocketman's 'I'm gonna love me again'," and later, the presentation of Oscars for Directing, Best Actor, Actress, and Best Picture. The golden, circular 'OSCARS' structure is illuminated as the announcer concludes, "The Oscars on ABC, brought to you by Cadillac." A woman in a black gown with a large white bow at the neckline then appears on stage, holding a card and speaking with a joyful expression, greeting the audience with "Good evening" and announcing the nominees for Best International Feature Film. This leads into a cinematic montage of the nominees, accompanied by the woman's voice: a close-up of a man’s intense blue eyes from 'Corpus Christi' (Poland), directed by Jan Komasa; a woman in a yellow top tending to beehives in 'Honeyland' (North Macedonia), directed by Ljubo Stefanov and Tamara Kotevska; and finally, a group of people celebrating in front of the Eiffel Tower in 'Les Misérables' (France), directed by Ladj Ly. Throughout the celebratory and grand atmosphere, with dynamic lighting, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and on-screen text identifies each film’s title, country of origin, and director.

The segment opens with a cinematic montage, accompanied by a female voice announcing clips from international feature film nominees: 'Pain and Glory,' directed by Pedro Almodóvar, and 'Parasite' from South Korea, directed by Bong Joon Ho, with on-screen text identifying each. The scene transitions to the Oscars stage, where a large, illuminated 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE FILM' sign glows against a grand, circular, starry backdrop. The Woman in Black Gown with White Bow, dressed in a black gown with a prominent white bow, stands at the podium, her clear voice declaring, "And the Oscar goes to... Parasite!" as the audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, erupts in enthusiastic applause and cheers. She continues, noting, "This is the first movie from South Korea to be nominated and to win the Oscar." Bong Joon Ho, the director of 'Parasite,' is shown celebrating with Han Jin Won, his co-winner, in the audience before they rise and make their way to the stage. Bong Joon Ho, wearing a dark suit and glasses, accepts the golden Oscar statuette with a joyful expression, joined on stage by Han Jin Won and a Woman in Black Outfit, who also holds an Oscar. Bong Joon Ho delivers an emotional acceptance speech, first expressing gratitude in English with a Korean accent, saying, "Thank you. Ah, great honor," then speaking in Korean, which is subsequently interpreted. He raises the statuette triumphantly as the audience continues to cheer, with celebratory orchestral music playing throughout. The stage backdrop features a swirling, illuminated design, and the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, emphasizing the grandeur of the moment.

The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, a man in a black suit and glasses, holding an Oscar statuette, speaking into a microphone on stage. Beside him stands Han Jin Won, also in a dark suit, holding an Oscar, listening attentively, smiling, and nodding. The stage's dramatic backdrop features a swirling blue design with glowing golden lights. Bong Joon Ho's Korean speech is translated by a female voice, expressing his happiness as the first recipient of the "International Feature Film" award under its new name and acknowledging the new direction this change symbolizes. He then thanks the "wonderful actors and staff" who made the film, specifically naming beloved cast members like Song Kang-ho, Lee Sun-kyun, Choi Woo-shik, Jang Hye-jin, Park So-dam, and Lee Jung-eun. As the translator relays his gratitude to the "loving crew members and cast members," the camera cuts to a wide shot of the audience, showing enthusiastic clapping and cheers from notable figures such as Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, all in formal attire and smiling warmly. The loud applause and cheers continue as the camera returns to Bong Joon Ho, who continues his speech, thanking cinematographer Hong Kyung-pyo, production designer Lee Ha-jun, editor Yang Jin-mo, and all the artists, a sentiment echoed by the translator. He concludes by thanking Barunson, CJ, and Neon for helping realize their vision. Han Jin Won claps in support throughout. An ABC logo is visible in the bottom right, while promotional overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear in the top left, indicating a fan-edited compilation. The overall atmosphere is one of triumph and global celebration for 'Parasite.'

event_blocks.summary: Bong Joon Ho, beaming and emotional, held an Oscar statuette on a grand stage with an illuminated circular backdrop, thanking "everyone at CJ and who allowed me to actualize my vision," before adding, "I'm ready, ready to drink tonight," eliciting laughter and applause, and concluding with "Until next morning. Thank you." The audience, including a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown and a woman in a gold sequined gown, cheered enthusiastically as Bong Joon Ho raised his Oscar. The scene shifted to an award presentation where a woman in a green off-the-shoulder satin gown announced, "And the Oscar goes to... Hildur Guðnadóttir for Joker," as celebratory music erupted and the audience, including a woman in a black sequined gown and a man in a black tuxedo, applauded. The woman in green noted, "This is the first Oscar nomination for Hildur Guðnadóttir," as the audience, including a woman in a black dress with a choker necklace, stood and applauded. The segment concluded with a wide shot of the stage, the presenters, and the applauding audience against the backdrop, with the ABC logo visible. Separately, in a dimly lit room, on-screen text displayed 'JOKER' by Hildur Guðnadóttir with eerie orchestral music. At a grand awards ceremony, Hildur Guðnadóttir, in a black gown with a sparkling silver sequined bodice, walked to the stage to accept her Oscar, thanking the Academy and fellow nominees, stating, "A film composer is only as creative as the dialogue with their director. Todd Phillips, we're so..." The stage backdrop featured a red and gold gradient, with the 'ORIGINAL SCORE' award for 'JOKER' identified on-screen. The audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, applauded warmly. Hildur, radiant in her embellished gown, thanked her husband, mother, and son, Koden, before delivering a powerful message to "the girls, to the women, to the mothers, to the daughters who hear the music bubbling within," urging them to "please speak up, we need to hear your voices," which was met with sustained, roaring applause and cheers, despite an illuminated 'ORIGINAL SONG' sign above the stage.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, wearing a black suit and glasses, beaming with emotion as he holds an Oscar statuette on a grand stage with a circular, illuminated backdrop. He thanks "everyone at CJ and who allowed me to actualize my vision," followed by loud applause and cheering from the packed audience. As the camera pulls back to show the full stage, he adds, "I'm ready, ready to drink tonight," eliciting laughter and more applause before he concludes, "Until next morning. Thank you." The audience, including a smiling woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown and a clapping woman in a gold sequined gown, continues to cheer enthusiastically as Bong Joon Ho raises his Oscar in triumph. The scene transitions to a different award presentation, where three women stand on stage: one in a shimmering silver gown, one in a green off-the-shoulder satin gown, and one in a black lace top with a pink skirt. The woman in green, holding a card, speaks into the microphone, announcing, "And the Oscar goes to... Hildur Guðnadóttir for Joker." Immediate applause and celebratory music erupt from the audience, which includes a clapping woman in a black sequined gown and a man in a black tuxedo. The woman in green continues, stating, "This is the first Oscar nomination for Hildur Guðnadóttir," as the audience, now standing and applauding, including a woman in a black dress with a choker necklace, celebrates. The segment ends with a wide shot of the stage, the three women, and the applauding audience against the grand backdrop, with the ABC logo visible.

The video opens with a dark, close-up shot of a man's face, transitioning to a dimly lit room where a man in a white shirt and dark jacket stands near a glowing blue screen, displaying the on-screen text 'JOKER' by Hildur Guðnadóttir, accompanied by orchestral music with an eerie string sound. The scene shifts to a grand awards ceremony in a large, ornate theater filled with a formally dressed audience, where a woman, Hildur Guðnadóttir, in a black gown with a sparkling silver sequined bodice, walks confidently to the stage, holding an Oscar statuette. As she begins her emotional acceptance speech, the audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, applauds warmly and cheers loudly. Promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' are visible in the top left corner. Close-ups capture Hildur’s emotional expression as she speaks, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right corner. Her voice, filled with gratitude, states, "This is so touching. Thank you, the Academy for welcoming me so warmly. It's just, I don't even, I don't know what to say. My fellow nominees, masters of the craft, it's been such an honor to get to know you all. You're so special. A film composer is only as creative as the dialogue with their director. Todd Phillips, we're so..." The stage backdrop is a warm gradient of red and gold, and on-screen text identifies the award as 'ORIGINAL SCORE' for 'JOKER' by Hildur Guðnadóttir. The audience continues to cheer and applaud, and Hildur smiles gratefully, holding the statuette close. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the audience standing and applauding, emphasizing the celebratory atmosphere of the event.

The segment opens with a close-up of Hildur Guðnadóttir, radiant in a black gown with a heavily embellished silver sequined bodice, her short dark hair in an updo, as she stands at a grand, gold-accented podium, holding her Oscar statuette. Her voice, filled with emotion and gratitude, begins her acceptance speech for the Original Score award for 'Joker'. She thanks her family, her 'incredible husband Sam,' her 'love, my best friend, my other set of ears,' and her mother and son Koden, expressing profound love with a joyful laugh. Throughout her speech, the audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, are seen smiling warmly and applauding enthusiastically. The stage is bathed in a warm red and gold gradient, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right corner. On-screen promotional text overlays, such as 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE,' appear in the top left. Hildur then delivers a powerful message to 'the girls, to the women, to the mothers, to the daughters who hear the music bubbling within,' urging them to 'please speak up, we need to hear your voices,' which is met with sustained, roaring applause and cheers. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the stage, the large 'ORIGINAL SONG' sign illuminated above (despite the award being for Original Score), as the audience continues their joyous applause, celebrating the prestigious moment.

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Host), now in a purple suit, presented the nominees for Directing, including Martin Scorsese, Todd Phillips, Sam Mendes, Quentin Tarantino, and Bong Joon Ho, with behind-the-scenes footage of each director shown. Following the announcement of Bong Joon Ho as the winner, he ascended the stage, visibly emotional, to accept the award. He began his speech in Korean, translated by an off-screen voice and a woman in a black outfit, expressing gratitude and sharing a quote from Martin Scorsese: "the most personal is the most creative." He also recounted his surprise at winning, having believed his day was done after winning Best International Feature, and mentioned his admiration for Scorsese, Todd Phillips, and Sam Mendes, humorously wishing to split the Oscar trophy. The audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, responded with enthusiastic applause, cheering, and warm smiles throughout the emotional and celebratory segment, with the ABC logo consistently visible.

Bong Joon Ho.description: A man with short black hair and glasses, wearing a black tuxedo, standing on stage, visibly emotional, wiping his face, and holding an Oscar statuette while delivering an acceptance speech in Korean, occasionally speaking English with an accent (e.g., 'Quentin, I love you'). He expresses a humorous desire to split the Oscar with a 'Texas chainsaw' if allowed.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dark, sweeping shot of a grand, circular stage illuminated by golden light streaks and a starry backdrop, transitioning to a wide view of the audience in a dimly lit auditorium, filled with the sound of applause, cheering, and whooping. The camera then focuses on the Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Host), now dressed in a distinctive purple suit with yellow trim, a black bow tie, a black hat, and purple-rimmed glasses, standing at a microphone on the stage. He speaks animatedly, gesturing with his right hand, saying, "Last time I was on this stage, I had the honor of jumping to arms by Morehouse brother, Mr. Samuel Jackson," followed by laughter and applause as he adds, "And thank God he didn't drop me." With a glowing blue Oscar statuette as a backdrop, he continues, "Stand up here tonight for hearing the nominees for achievement in directing," as applause and building orchestral music accompany his words. The scene cuts to a montage of behind-the-scenes footage from various films, introduced by the host's voice and accompanied by the continuing music and applause: Martin Scorsese for 'The Irishman', shown directing a scene; Todd Phillips for 'Joker', in a casual meeting; Sam Mendes for '1917', directing on a war-torn set; and Quentin Tarantino for 'Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood', directing on a sunny street. The montage concludes with Bong Joon Ho for 'Parasite', shown in a kitchen scene with a family. The segment culminates with a return to the stage, where the word 'DIRECTING' appears in large, illuminated letters above the host, who then presents the nominees for Best Director, repeating "Bong Joon Ho!" to particularly loud applause and cheering. A graphic displays the five nominees: Martin Scorsese, Todd Phillips, Sam Mendes, Quentin Tarantino, and Bong Joon Ho, each with their film title and a still image from the audience, while the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout.

The segment opens with a close-up of a nominee display screen showing 'MARTIN SCORSESE' and 'TODD PHILLIPS' for the Directing category, as enthusiastic applause and cheering erupt. A female announcer's voice declares, "This is the second Oscar tonight and the third nomination for Bong Joon Ho." The camera then cuts to the audience, where Bong Joon Ho, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, is seen rising from his seat, surrounded by clapping attendees including a woman in a green off-the-shoulder satin gown and a woman in a black lace top with a pink skirt. He makes his way through the crowd, smiling and waving, to the continued sound of fervent applause and cheering. A behind-the-scenes clip of Bong Joon Ho in a kitchen scene from 'Parasite' is shown with on-screen text, before he ascends the stage. There, he is greeted by a woman in a black outfit and a man in a dark suit (Han Jin Won), both smiling, against a deep blue, starry backdrop with swirling light patterns and a prominent 'DIRECTING' sign. Bong Joon Ho, now at the podium wearing a black suit and glasses, is visibly emotional, wiping his eyes and covering his face with his hand. He speaks, first saying "Thank you" in English, then continuing in Korean. A female translator's voice conveys his sentiment: "After winning Best International Feature, I thought I was done for the day and was ready to relax." The audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, is shown clapping and smiling warmly, as Bong Joon Ho continues speaking in Korean. Wide shots emphasize the grand, celebratory atmosphere of the tiered auditorium, with the ABC logo remaining in the bottom right corner.

The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, visibly emotional in a black tuxedo and glasses, wiping his eyes and covering his face at the podium on the Oscars stage, which features a dark, starry backdrop illuminated by blue and golden light streaks. The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right, while intermittent on-screen text overlays like 'Buy code' with a QR, 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO,' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear. Bong Joon Ho begins to speak in Korean, translated by an off-screen voice, expressing gratitude and recalling a saying he "carved deep into [his] heart: the most personal is the most creative." As he continues, the camera cuts to a wide shot of the formally dressed audience, where Tom Hanks claps warmly, followed by Al Pacino, also smiling and clapping. The atmosphere is celebratory, with many attendees standing and applauding enthusiastically. Bong Joon Ho then reveals, through his translator, that the quote is from "our great Martin Scorsese," which elicits a massive wave of cheers, whistles, and sustained applause from the crowd, further emphasizing the celebratory mood. The camera pans across the excited audience before returning to Bong Joon Ho, who, still emotional, continues in Korean, translated as him being "terrified watching Martin's films in school" and never expecting to win, considering it an honor just to be nominated alongside Scorsese. He gestures occasionally, standing on the circular stage with glowing white edges, as the lighting shifts between warm reds and cool blues. The sequence concludes with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, still visibly moved, as the audience's fervent cheering persists.

The video segment opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, visibly emotional, wiping his face with his hand as he stands on a grand awards ceremony stage with a dark, starry backdrop and blue lighting accents. Beside him, a woman in a black outfit, likely a co-winner or collaborator, stands smiling. The woman translates, stating it was 'a huge honor' and that she 'never thought I would win,' as Bong Joon Ho, wearing a black tuxedo and glasses, begins his acceptance speech in Korean. The camera cuts to the audience, showing Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, all smiling warmly and clapping enthusiastically. The focus returns to Bong Joon Ho, who continues his speech in Korean, which the woman translates, mentioning how 'Quentin always put my films on his list' when people in the US were unfamiliar with them, adding 'He's here. Thank you so much.' Bong Joon Ho then directly addresses 'Quentin' in English, saying 'I love you,' which elicits laughter and enthusiastic applause from the audience. He gestures with his hands, holding an Oscar statuette, and continues his speech in Korean. The translator conveys his humorous desire to get a 'Texas chainsaw' to 'split the Oscar trophy into five' and share it with 'Todd and Sam,' whom he admires, if the Academy allows. This prompts more laughter and applause from the audience, including Al Pacino and Tom Hanks, who react with visible delight. The atmosphere is celebratory and emotional, with the ABC logo consistently displayed in the bottom right corner.
