event_blocks.summary: The event commences with a black screen featuring a red and white promotional overlay reading 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE,' accompanied by a whimsical, upbeat instrumental tune characterized by a prominent flute and percussion. The scene shifts to a grand awards stage illuminated by warm ambient lighting, showcasing a circular golden design and sparkling vertical light strands. A woman, identified as the Woman in Navy Blue Gown, with short platinum blonde hair and wearing a dark navy velvet gown with cut-out shoulders and circular brooches, confidently approaches a central microphone, holding a white card. She begins speaking in a clear, engaging British accent, humorously remarking, "Winning an Oscar ages you," prompting laughter and applause from the audience. She expresses gratitude, stating, "Hello guys. Um, thank you for having me back. It's such a pleasure to be here. Last year was the best night of my husband's life," followed by the quip, "He actually says that. And I've given birth three times," eliciting further laughter and applause. Throughout her address, the ABC Logo is visible in the bottom right corner, while various on-screen text overlays, such as 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'TV 14 DLV', and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO,' appear in the top left. The woman, occasionally consulting her card, concludes by acknowledging the difficulty of finding a single thread connecting the performances, stating, "So I, I hope you're all having as good a time as he did. And I did. But um, I don't want to keep you from the bar much longer. I know I'm supposed to talk about acting and find some clever thread connecting these performances, but their beauty and brilliance surely lies in the fact that there isn't one. And I'm sorry." The celebratory atmosphere is amplified by the stage's grand backdrop, featuring a large, curved golden structure and glowing lights, as the whimsical music persists.

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens with a dark, blurred shot transitioning into a cinematic montage with an upbeat, slightly dramatic score, featuring a man in a dark suit with a serious expression in a dimly lit environment and a close-up of a hand holding a cigarette, as a male voice questions, "You didn't ask the same questions every week. That's your job." The scene shifts to a man in a white shirt with graying hair and a beard speaking earnestly in a warmly lit room, with on-screen text 'but really I don't hold a grudge,' and audio in Spanish, "Todos querían matarlo, pero realmente no le guardo rencor" (Everyone wanted to kill him, but really I don't hold a grudge), while a man with long dark hair in a black sweater listens intently. A female voice states, "This is the name. He needs to know that I fought for him." The setting changes to a bright, sunlit outdoor scene where a man in a denim jacket stands beside a 'Musso & Frank Grill' sign. The montage continues with a man in a light-colored shirt and sunglasses looking into the distance, followed by a man in a dark suit with a red tie passionately declaring, "This is the face of failure that is your crying about... Jesus built walls. His face is the face of mercy. Mercy is the dynamite that blows down walls." A calm male voice reflects on someone "coming to terms with what it's like to be slightly more..." The scene cuts to a man in a white robe shaking hands with another in a black robe with red trim in a grand hall. A man in a dark shirt is shown in a dimly lit room, followed by a man with graying hair and a beard in a green shirt with red trim speaking seriously, with on-screen text 'Control the emotion. Don't cry,' and audio in Spanish, "Controla la emoción. No llores. No es mejor actor el que llora, sino el que lucha por contener las lágrimas" (Control the emotion. Don't cry. The best actor is not the one who cries, but the one who fights to hold back tears). An agitated male voice expresses, "I don't know why everyone is so rude... I don't want anything from you. Maybe a little bit of warmth, maybe a hug..." The segment concludes with a close-up of a man with long dark hair in a black shirt looking distressed and holding his head in his hands as the background music swells and ends, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right corner.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with intense close-ups of a man's eyes, transitioning to a dramatic scene in a dimly lit room where a man in a red sweater aggressively yells, accompanied by a male voice expressing a desperate desire to ensure someone's well-being. This is followed by a solemn scene of a cardinal in a church, bowing his head as a calm voice speaks of truth and love. The setting then shifts to a dazzling awards ceremony stage with a starry backdrop and golden light streaks, where a montage of film characters, including the man in the red sweater, is displayed on a large screen as applause builds. A female announcer introduces the nominees for Actor in a Leading Role, panning to the audience where Antonio Banderas, Leonardo DiCaprio, Adam Driver, and Jonathan Pryce are shown. The segment concludes with the announcement that Joaquin Phoenix has won the Oscar for 'Joker,' prompting loud applause and cheering. Phoenix, visibly emotional, walks onto the stage amidst a standing ovation and celebratory music, beginning his acceptance speech for 'Actor in a Leading Role' for 'Joker.' He expresses humility, stating he feels "so much better at this right now" and is not "elevated above any of my fellow nominees or anyone in this room." He shares his love for film, calling it an "extraordinary life," and emphasizes the "greatest gift" is the "opportunity to use our voice for the voiceless," reflecting on "distressing issues that we are facing collectively." Phoenix speaks with a solemn, reflective tone about commonality in championing causes, stating, "whether we're talking about gender inequality or racism or queer rights or indigenous rights or animal rights, we're talking about the fight against injustice," fighting "the belief that one nation, one people, one race, one gender, or one species has the right to dominate, control, and use and exploit another with impunity." He further discusses humanity's disconnection from the natural world and an "egocentric worldview," plundering resources. He advocates for using "love and compassion as our guiding principles" to "create, develop and implement systems of change that are beneficial to all sentient beings and to the environment." Phoenix concludes with a personal reflection, admitting, "I've been selfish, I've been cruel at times, hard to work with," but expresses gratitude for second chances, believing "that's when we're at our best when we support..." Throughout the ceremony, the ABC logo and various on-screen promotional texts, including a 'Buy code' QR code and subscription prompts, are visible. Notable audience members like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt are shown clapping warmly.

He passionately declares, "whether we're talking about gender inequality or racism or queer rights or indigenous rights or animal rights, we're talking about the fight against injustice." He elaborates on this, fighting "the belief that one nation, one people, one race, one gender, or one species has the right to dominate, control, and use and exploit another with impunity." The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner. The camera alternates between tight close-ups of his eyes and mouth, capturing his emotional delivery, and wider shots of the vast, formally dressed audience, including attentive figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt. A powerful, sustained applause erupts from the audience, their hands raised in appreciation, creating a powerful sense of shared recognition. As the applause subsides, Phoenix continues, stating, "we've become very disconnected from the natural world and many of us, what we're guilty of is an egocentric worldview. The belief that we're the center of the universe. We go into the natural world and we plunder it for its resources." He concludes the segment by saying, "We feel..."

The video opens with a dark, blurred shot that quickly transitions to a wide view of a grand awards ceremony stage, where a formally dressed audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, is seated in tiered rows, clapping warmly to the faint orchestral music. Joaquin Phoenix, a man with graying, wavy hair and a beard, wearing a black tuxedo with a bow tie, walks onto the stage and stands at a microphone, his expression solemn and reflective. He begins his acceptance speech for the Actor in a Leading Role award for 'Joker', speaking with intense emotion about animal cruelty, stating, "we artificially inseminate a cow, and when she gives birth, we steal her baby... we take her milk that's intended for her calf, and we put it in our coffee and our cereal." He then transitions to human nature, asserting, "we fear the idea of personal change because we think that we have to sacrifice something to give something up," but emphasizes that "human beings at our best are so inventive and creative and ingenious." As he continues, the camera alternates between close-ups of his face, capturing his intense gaze and emotional delivery, and wider shots showing him on the stage with a backdrop of golden, swirling lights and a large, circular, illuminated design. He gestures with his hands, occasionally touching his face or adjusting his hair, as he advocates for using "love and compassion as our guiding principles" to "create, develop and implement systems of change that are beneficial to all sentient beings and to the environment." He concludes this segment with a personal reflection, admitting, "I've been selfish, I've been cruel at times, hard to work with," but expresses gratitude that "so many of you in this room have given me a second chance," believing "that's when we're at our best when we support..." On-screen text overlays, including a 'Buy code' QR code and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE', appear intermittently, while the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, all contributing to an atmosphere of prestige and emotional weight.

event_blocks.summary: Joaquin Phoenix, accepting an award in a black tuxedo on an illuminated stage with a starry backdrop, delivered an emotional speech about humanity's best, citing his brother's lyric, "Run to the rescue with love, and peace will follow," to loud applause from attendees including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt. The scene then shifted to the Best Actress nominees announcement, with Cynthia Erivo, Scarlett Johansson, Saoirse Ronan, Charlize Theron, and Renée Zellweger all shown clapping as their names were called. Ultimately, Renée Zellweger won the Oscar for 'Actress in a Leading Role' for 'Judy,' ascending the grand stage with a circular, illuminated backdrop and golden light streaks to accept her award. Visibly emotional and tearful, Zellweger, in a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, thanked the Academy, her fellow nominees, her family, and the cast and crew, reflecting on the importance of celebrating heroes like Judy Garland and how they unite people. She specifically honored figures such as Neil Armstrong, Sally Ride, Dolores Huerta, Venus and Serena, Selena, Bob Dylan, Scorsese, Fred Rogers, Harriet Tubman, and military personnel, emphasizing unity and the American Dream. Throughout the broadcast, the ABC logo and promotional text overlays were visible.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Joaquin Phoenix, dressed in a black tuxedo, delivering an emotional acceptance speech from a grand, illuminated stage with a starry backdrop. His voice, slightly raspy and reflective, speaks of helping each other grow and guiding towards redemption, stating, "That is the best of humanity." This is met with a wave of loud, sustained applause from the audience, including visible figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, who are seen smiling and clapping warmly. Phoenix continues, sharing a personal anecdote about his brother, who at 17, wrote the lyric, "Run to the rescue with love, and peace will follow," concluding with a heartfelt "Thank you." Another round of enthusiastic applause erupts as the camera captures his solemn, intense expression and subtle gestures. The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the stage displaying a cinematic montage, before cutting to Cynthia Erivo, elegantly dressed in a white one-shoulder gown with a jeweled headpiece, clapping attentively. A clear female voice then announces, "Here are the nominees for performance by an actress in a leading role," specifically naming "Cynthia Erivo, Harriet," which is followed by further applause. Throughout, promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and a QR code, along with the ABC logo, are visible, indicating a broadcast or fan-edited compilation of this prestigious, celebratory event.

The segment opens with a close-up of Cynthia Erivo, her face illuminated by stage lights, wearing a white one-shoulder gown and jeweled headpiece, clapping with a serious expression, as an unseen announcer's voice declares "Scarlett Johansson, Marriage Story," followed by applause and upbeat orchestral music. The camera then cuts to Scarlett Johansson, in a shimmering silver gown, clapping and smiling radiantly, her name having just been announced. The scene shifts to Saoirse Ronan, in a black sleeveless gown, clapping with a warm smile as the announcer states "Saoirse Ronan, Little Women," with continued applause and music. Charlize Theron, in a black off-the-shoulder gown, is then seen clapping with a composed expression as the announcer says "Charlize Theron, Bombshell." Renée Zellweger, in a light gray one-shoulder gown, claps with a serene smile as her name, "Renée Zellweger, Judy," is announced. A graphic overlay displays all the nominees for 'Actress in a Leading Role': Cynthia Erivo ('Harriet'), Scarlett Johansson ('Marriage Story'), Saoirse Ronan ('Little Women'), Charlize Theron ('Bombshell'), and Renée Zellweger ('Judy'), accompanied by the ongoing applause and music. The announcer then dramatically declares, "The Oscar goes to... Renée Zellweger!" prompting a loud cheer and a swell of triumphant orchestral music as Renée Zellweger stands up, her expression joyful, and is embraced by a man in a black tuxedo. She then walks down the aisle, smiling and waving to the audience, while the announcer adds, "This is the second Oscar and fourth nomination for Renée Zellweger." As she ascends the grand stage, featuring a circular, illuminated backdrop with golden light streaks, the audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, is shown clapping enthusiastically amidst the celebratory music and cheers. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner.

The video opens with a black screen before transitioning to a grand awards ceremony stage, where Renée Zellweger, identified by a lower-third graphic as the winner of 'Actress in a Leading Role' for 'Judy', stands at a microphone holding an Oscar statuette. Wearing a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, she is bathed in warm, golden bokeh lights against a dark backdrop, creating a celebratory and elegant atmosphere. With a voice filled with emotion, gratitude, and a hint of tears, Renée thanks "the Academy" and expresses her appreciation for being invited to such a "special" and "meaningful" experience. The camera alternates between close-ups of her tearful eyes and smiling lips, and wider shots revealing the grand stage with its circular golden design and tiered audience, who are seen clapping and watching attentively. She acknowledges her fellow nominees, "Cynthia, Scarlett, Charlize, Saoirse," stating it's "an honor to be considered in your company." The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, while promotional text like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and a QR code appear in the top left. Renée continues, thanking "David Livingston, Cameron McCracken, Director Rupert Goold," expressing pride in working on the "beautiful movie." She then extends her gratitude to "my stage moms, Matt, Paul, Gary, Jessie Buckley, Finn Wittrock, Rufus Sewell," and "all cast and the crew who worked so hard on this film to celebrate Judy Garland," before concluding with thanks to her "extended family at CAA, Peter Bryan and Kevin." The overall mood is one of heartfelt triumph and celebration.

A visibly emotional Renée Zellweger, her eyes glistening with tears, stands at a podium in a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, her blonde hair in an elegant updo, holding an Oscar statuette. As she speaks into the microphone, her expression shifts between joy, gratitude, and emotion, occasionally wiping her eyes. Her voice, filled with heartfelt emotion, thanks "Nancy Ryder" for 25 years and "John Carabino," her "handsome date of 25 years," for always "dreaming bigger" and bringing it back to "work, joy, and gratitude." She acknowledges her "immigrant folks who came here with nothing but each other and a belief in the American Dream," and extends gratitude to "Big Brother Drew, Randy, Judy, Stone, and Ava" for "all the love and support possible." She reflects that the past year of conversations celebrating Judy Garland "across genders, generations, and cultures" has been a "really cool reminder that our heroes unite us." The background features dark, golden bokeh lights and a grand, circular stage backdrop with golden light streaks, enhancing the celebratory mood. Wide shots reveal a formally dressed audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, all clapping and smiling warmly. Throughout, the ABC logo is displayed in the bottom right, while promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and a QR code appear in the top left.

The segment opens from a black screen, transitioning to a close-up of Renée Zellweger, who stands emotionally at a podium on a grand stage, holding her Oscar statuette. She is resplendent in a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, her blonde hair styled in an elegant updo, as the stage glows with warm, golden lights. A large, curved backdrop prominently displays 'BEST ACTRESS' in bold, glowing letters, while a softly lit image of her face adds a personal touch behind her. With tearful eyes and subtle gestures, Renée delivers her acceptance speech, her voice filled with emotion as she speaks of those who “inspire us to find the best in ourselves.” The camera alternates between intimate close-ups of her expressive face and wider shots revealing the vast, darkened, tiered audience, all captivated by her words. She continues, listing a diverse array of heroes like “Neil Armstrong, Sally Ride, Dolores Huerta, Venus and Serena, and Selena, Bob Dylan, Scorsese, Fred Rogers, Harriet Tubman,” emphasizing how “we agree on our teachers” and “our courageous men and women in uniform who serve,” including “first responders and firefighters.” She explains that “when we celebrate our heroes, we are reminded of who we are as one people, united.” The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout. Renée concludes by noting that “Judy Garland did not receive this honor in her time,” but she is “certain that this moment is an extension of the celebration of her legacy.” The atmosphere is both celebratory and intimate, with the spotlight effect underscoring the significance of her presence.

event_blocks.summary: Orchestral music begins with a close-up of a Man in Dark Suit, as male voices declare, 'We're going to war with these people,' and 'But nobody's like this offer.' The scene shifts to a nighttime cityscape with an illuminated building and a parking lot of yellow taxis, then to a chaotic crowd inside a dimly lit hall with people holding signs and shouting. A female voice announces, 'The Irishman,' as a male voice lists producers, coinciding with a Man in Suit with Red Tie holding a phone to his ear with a serious expression. The narrative shifts to a Young Boy in Hitler Youth Uniform interacting with a Woman in Patterned Jacket in a Nazi-era town square, as female voices assert, 'He's not a Nazi,' and 'I'm surrounded by swastikas, so I think that's a pretty good sign right there.' A male voice shouts, 'Do not let your German mind be bossed around!' as the boy appears distressed in a bedroom with the woman behind him, prompting a female voice to respond, 'I won't let her boss my German brain around,' and another to add, 'Definitely not a good time to be a Nazi.' A Man in Suit with Glasses is seen seated at a table, writing, before a female voice announces, 'Jojo Rabbit,' and a male voice lists producers. The video then introduces a Man with Green Hair and Clown Makeup, smiling widely, as male voices ask, 'Tell us a joke,' and 'What do you get... when you cross a mentally ill loner with a society that abandons him and treats him like trash?' A close-up of a Woman with Dark Hair, her expression intense, is shown as a female voice shouts, 'I'll tell you what you get! You get what you deserve!' The final frames feature the clown-faced man in a red suit, surrounded by a cheering crowd, with On-Screen Promotional Text identifying the film as 'Joker' and listing producers, while a female voice begins to speak about women having 'minds, and... souls, as well as just hearts. And they've got ambition and they've got...' The ABC Logo is visible throughout.

raw_segments: Upbeat, slightly tense orchestral music begins as the segment opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit, his face partially obscured, creating a mysterious atmosphere while male voices declare, 'We're going to war with these people,' and 'But nobody's like this offer.' The scene quickly transitions to a nighttime cityscape featuring an illuminated building and a parking lot of yellow taxis, followed by a chaotic crowd inside a dimly lit hall, where people are holding signs and shouting. A female voice then announces, 'The Irishman,' as a male voice lists 'Martin Scorsese, Robert De Niro, Jane Rosenthal, and Emma Tillinger Koskoff, producers,' coinciding with a man in a suit holding a phone to his ear with a serious expression, indicating high stakes. The narrative shifts to a young boy in a Hitler Youth uniform, looking confused or fearful, interacting with a woman in a patterned coat in a Nazi-era town square, as female voices assert, 'He's not a Nazi,' and 'I'm surrounded by swastikas, so I think that's a pretty good sign right there.' A male voice shouts, 'Do not let your German mind be bossed around!' as the boy appears distressed in a bedroom with the woman behind him, prompting a female voice to respond, 'I won't let her boss my German brain around,' and another to add, 'Definitely not a good time to be a Nazi.' A man in a suit with glasses is then seen seated at a table, writing, before a female voice announces, 'Jojo Rabbit,' and a male voice lists 'Carthew Neal, Taika Waititi, Chelsea Winstanley, producers.' The video then introduces a man with green hair and clown makeup, smiling widely, as male voices ask, 'Tell us a joke,' and 'What do you get... when you cross a mentally ill loner with a society that abandons him and treats him like trash?' A close-up of a woman with dark hair, her expression intense, is shown as a female voice shouts, 'I'll tell you what you get! You get what you deserve!' The final frames feature the clown-faced man in a red suit, surrounded by a cheering crowd, with on-screen text identifying the film as 'Joker' and listing producers Todd Phillips, Bradley Cooper, and Emma Tillinger Koskoff, while a female voice begins to speak about women having 'minds, and... souls, as well as just hearts. And they've got ambition and they've got...' The ABC logo is visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative synthesizes cinematic clips with the 'Parasite' Best Picture Oscar win. Initial scenes depict dramatic moments from 'Little Women,' featuring a woman in a yellow dress declaring, "Love is just beauty, and I'm so sick of people saying that love is just all a woman is for. I'm so sick of it," followed by outdoor gatherings and a kitchen scene from 'Marriage Story' with dialogue about relationships and selfishness. A stark transition to a war-torn battlefield from '1917' shows a soldier crawling through mud amidst explosions, followed by a reflective male voice stating, "journey is over. I think we've reached the end of the trail cliff." The video then shifts to the 'Parasite' storyline, beginning with a tense scene in a doorway and a man with a gun, then a clip from 'Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood' with a man in a blue jacket being attacked. Chaos ensues in a dimly lit alley with sounds of struggle and Korean dialogue, leading to the announcement of 'Parasite' winning Best Picture at the Oscars. Bong Joon Ho and his team, including Han Jin Won and a Woman in Black Outfit, accept the award amidst cheers, confetti, and emotional speeches. The acceptance speeches, delivered in Korean and translated to English, express immense gratitude to the Academy, the Korean film audience, and the cast and crew, with specific praise for director Bong Joon Ho's character and directorial style. The ABC logo and promotional text are visible throughout the segment, underscoring the blend of cinematic previews and the triumphant awards ceremony.

Film Scene from '1917'.description: A soldier in a muddy trench during World War I, with on-screen text reading '1917' and 'SAM MENDES, PIPPA HARRIS, JAYNE-ANN TENGGRREN AND CALLUM MCDOUGALL PRODUCERS', introduced by an announcer stating, '1917. Sam Mendes, Pippa Harris, Jayne-Ann Tenggren, and Callum McDougall, producers,' and accompanied by intense war sounds.

You don't need to identify it as selfishness anymore!'

raw_segments: The video segment opens with a dark, indistinct frame before transitioning to a period scene featuring a woman in a yellow dress with a ruffled collar in a warmly lit, ornate interior, as a passionate female voice declares, "Love is just beauty, and I'm so sick of people saying that love is just all a woman is for. I'm so sick of it." This is followed by an outdoor grassy field where a man in a vest and white shirt walks toward a woman in a brown coat, then a lively outdoor gathering with people in period attire celebrating around a cake. On-screen text identifies the film as 'LITTLE WOMEN' and an announcer states, "Little Women. Amy Pascal, producer." The narrative then cuts to a modern kitchen where a man in a white shirt holds a piece of paper, followed by a man in a dark suit standing in a minimalist room with a coffee maker. A distressed female voice is heard saying, "I don't want to be married," followed by a calm male voice reflecting, "I'll never get to really be his parents again. It'll be different. It won't exist." A woman in a blue top and beige cardigan appears, gesturing expressively in a bright, modern dining area, as an accusing female voice states, "You wouldn't love me as much as I love you," met by a defensive male voice asking, "What does that have to do with LA?" and an angry female voice asserting, "You're so merged with your own selfishness! You don't need to identify it as selfishness anymore!" An announcer then identifies this segment as "Marriage Story." The segment abruptly shifts to a war-torn battlefield, showing a soldier in a brown uniform crawling through mud and debris amidst explosions and gunfire, then running across a desolate field as other soldiers follow. On-screen text identifies the film as '1917' and an announcer states, "1917. Sam Mendes, Pippa Harris, Jayne-Ann Tenggren, and Callum McDougall, producers." A calm male voice reflects, "journey is over. I think we've reached the end of the trail cliff," followed by an intense female voice exclaiming, "You are the devil! And I'm here to do the devil's..." The final frame shows a man in a dark shirt sitting in a dimly lit room, possibly reflecting on the events, with a pitcher on a table beside him. Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and promotional text overlays appear in the top left, including 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE'.

The video opens with a tense, darkly lit scene from 'Parasite,' featuring a woman in a striped top and denim shorts in a doorway, followed by a man with a gun, accompanied by a gun cocking sound and a woman's voice speaking Korean, saying "Now it's dumber than that." The scene quickly shifts to a man in a blue jacket being attacked, with on-screen text crediting 'Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood' and its producers, which a male voice reads aloud: "Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood. David Heyman, Shannon McIntosh, and Quentin Tarantino producers." The narrative then cuts to a modern kitchen where a man in a gray shirt is startled, and a woman in a black hoodie appears on a video intercom, stating in Korean, "I forgot something in the basement under the kitchen," before the scene erupts into chaos with people running through a dimly lit alley, accompanied by sounds of struggle and more Korean dialogue, including the names "Kwak Shin Ae and Bong Joon Ho producers" being read. The video transitions to the Oscars stage, where a woman with short platinum hair in a shimmering red gown, identified as the Woman in Red Ruffled Gown, stands at a podium under a golden, starry backdrop. With a dramatic pause, she announces, "And the Oscar goes to... Parasite!" as the audience erupts in cheers and celebratory music swells. The camera captures the ecstatic reactions of the cast and crew, including Bong Joon Ho, who is visibly emotional, wiping his face as he and his team rise to accept the award. The Woman in Red Ruffled Gown continues, stating, "Parasite has six Academy Award nominations and is the first film not in the English language..." The atmosphere shifts from suspenseful cinematic clips to a celebratory, grand awards ceremony, culminating in the triumphant moment of 'Parasite' winning Best Picture, with the ABC logo and promotional text visible throughout.

The video opens with a close-up of a woman, then cuts to a grand awards ceremony audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, clapping enthusiastically as an off-screen female announcer declares the winner is "winning four Oscars tonight." The camera pans across the joyful crowd before shifting to the stage, where a large golden Oscar statue is illuminated behind a circular, starry backdrop prominently displaying 'BEST PICTURE'. A woman in a red gown and a man in a black tuxedo stand at the podium as the sustained applause and cheering continue. The winners of 'Parasite' then take the stage, led by a visibly emotional Bong Joon Ho, who wipes his face and embraces his team, including Han Jin Won and a Woman in Black Outfit, as they celebrate with hugs and tears, holding their Oscar statuettes. The audience continues to cheer, with close-ups of smiling faces, including a Woman in White Suit and a bald woman in a white top, who is heard saying "Thank you. I'm speechless" before Bong Joon Ho speaks in Korean. The stage is bathed in golden light, with sparkling vertical strands and a grand, circular architectural design, creating a celebratory and prestigious atmosphere, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right corner throughout.

The video opens with a close-up of a woman with long dark hair, wearing a black outfit, speaking emotionally into a microphone on stage while holding an Oscar statuette. She begins her acceptance speech for 'Parasite' in Korean, expressing immense happiness, which is immediately translated into English: "We never imagined this to ever happen. We are so happy." Surrounding her is the visibly joyful and celebratory cast and crew, including a woman in a shimmering light-pink one-shoulder gown with a sparkling necklace and a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, all smiling and clapping amidst loud audience applause and cheering. The camera alternates between close-ups of the speaker and wider shots of the group, capturing their gratitude and excitement under a golden, starry backdrop with a prominent 'BEST PICTURE' sign. The speaker continues in Korean, translated as, "At this moment, something very meaningful and symbolic, and a starting point, history has been written... is happening right now," followed by more enthusiastic applause. She then conveys her "deepest gratitude and respect for all the members of the Academy for making this decision," both in Korean and English, as the audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, claps warmly. Intermittent on-screen text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO,' 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE,' and a QR code labeled 'Buy code' appear. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the entire group standing together under the grand, circular, golden architectural design, with the audience in the foreground cheering and applauding, as celebratory music swells.

The segment opens on a grand stage with a woman in a red sequined gown delivering a speech at a podium, set against a swirling golden backdrop. The scene quickly expands to a wide shot, revealing a large, illuminated golden Oscar statue at the stage's center, surrounded by a celebratory group. The audience erupts in enthusiastic cheers and applause, a sound that continues throughout the segment, with close-ups capturing the joyful reactions of attendees like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, all clapping warmly. The focus returns to the stage where a group, including Bong Joon Ho and Han Jin Won, celebrates amidst a shower of confetti. A woman in a black sequined gown, holding an Oscar statuette, steps to the microphone and begins an emotional acceptance speech, her voice filled with gratitude as she exclaims, "Hi everybody!" She specifically thanks "director Bong," adding, "Thank you for being you." She affectionately praises him, saying, "I like everything about him, his smile, his crazy hair, the way he talks, the way he walks, and especially the way he directs." She further highlights his character, noting, "what I really like about him is his sense of humor and the fact is he can be really making fun of himself and he never takes himself seriously." The stage is bathed in golden lights and sparkling effects, enhancing the jubilant atmosphere as the group, including Bong Joon Ho and Han Jin Won, smiles and cheers alongside her. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, while an on-screen promotional text in the top left corner reads 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' with a 'Buy code' QR code.

The segment opens with a dark, starry background, transitioning to a close-up of a golden Oscar statuette glowing amidst falling light particles, setting a celebratory tone. Over a backdrop of light, celebratory background music and applause, a female voice with a distinct accent immediately begins to express gratitude, stating, 'I'd like to thank everybody who's been supporting Parasite and who's been working with Parasite and who's been loving Parasite.' The scene shifts to the audience, where Brad Pitt, a man with long dark hair in a black velvet tuxedo, is seen clapping warmly, his expression engaged and celebratory as the speech continues. The camera then shows a wide shot of the stage where the Woman in Black Sequined Gown, with red hair, stands at a microphone, flanked by several formally dressed individuals, including men in black tuxedos (one with glasses, another with a bow tie) and a woman in a black outfit holding an Oscar statuette. She continues her emotional acceptance speech, thanking her brother, 'Jay,' for always supporting their dreams, even when it seemed impossible. The audience listens intently, including a Woman with Short Gray Hair and Hoop Earrings and a Young Man in Tuxedo. The speaker then passionately thanks 'our Korean film audience, our moviegoers,' for their unwavering support and honest opinions, which prevented complacency and pushed them to 'keep pushing the envelopes.' As she gestures expressively, the camera cuts back to the audience, showing a Woman in Shimmering Silver Gown laughing joyfully, and Cynthia Erivo, a woman with a shaved head and white gown, clapping with a serious and attentive expression. The Woman in Black Sequined Gown concludes by reiterating that 'without you, our Korean film audience, we are not here,' as the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, underscoring the atmosphere of triumph and deep emotion.

event_blocks.summary: The scene opens with a close-up of a man in a tuxedo, followed by a wide shot of a group on stage, including a woman with curly red hair in a sequined outfit at a microphone. The camera then reveals a grand stage with a golden, circular backdrop featuring sparkling lights and an illuminated Oscar statuette, as golden confetti falls. A woman with short, platinum blonde hair, wearing a shimmering red gown with sequin detailing and red gloves, stands at a podium and addresses the audience, saying, "Academy Awards! Congratulations to all the nominees and the winners. Good night, and thank you for watching." The camera zooms in on her expressive face before cutting to a wide shot of the stage where 'MARRIOTT BONVOY' is displayed on the backdrop, accompanied by a voiceover stating, "Hotel accommodations provided by Marriott Bonvoy." The ABC logo is consistently visible in the bottom right corner, and promotional text like 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' and a QR code appear in the top left. The event is bathed in warm, golden lighting, with the audience clapping and cheering in the foreground.
